policy_clause: Most items can be returned for a refund or replacement/exchange within 30 days of delivery as long as they are in original or unused condition.
Ambiguity_description: The phrase 'original or unused condition' is not defined within the policy. While there is mention of tags, packaging, and hygiene seals later, the standards for 'unused' or what constitutes having 'obvious signs of use' remain subjective. This creates challenges for agents determining eligibility or for automating rejection/acceptance of return requests.
List_of_ambiguities: ["'Original or unused condition' lacks a concrete definition, permitting divergent interpretations between customer, agent, and system.", 'Borderline scenarios (such as limited item use, light wear, or opened packaging) are not addressed, resulting in workflow friction for return eligibility determination.']
Intent: Highlight vagueness in condition requirements for return eligibility.

policy_clause: A refund will be provided if Amazon (or the third-party seller) has received the item, and determined that you are eligible for a refund. It can take up to 30 days for us to receive and process your return. In certain circumstances refund timeframes may be longer.
Ambiguity_description: While a nominal 30-day processing time is stated, the clause 'in certain circumstances refund timeframes may be longer' is undefined—lacking detail about the criteria for extensions or how customers/agents are notified if their case falls under this. This ambiguity hinders automated timeline computation and customer expectation-setting.
List_of_ambiguities: ["No specifics on what constitutes 'certain circumstances' warranting a longer refund timeframe.", 'Lack of maximum processing window or escalation criteria if 30 days is exceeded leads to automation gaps.']
Intent: Surface uncertainty in the upper bound and rules for processing refunds.

policy_clause: You may be charged a fee when you return an item that is damaged, missing parts, not in original condition, has tags removed or have obvious signs of use for reasons not due to an Amazon.com or seller error. | Up to 50% of the item price, except for Luxury Stores items, which will be charged 100% of the item price.
Ambiguity_description: It is not clear what the exact criteria, thresholds, or examples are for 'damaged,' 'missing parts,' 'not in original condition,' or especially 'obvious signs of use.' The phrase 'up to 50%' further introduces ambiguity as to the scale, criteria, and process for fee calculation. Automated agents would not have a rubric for classifying item state and fee assignment.
List_of_ambiguities: ["Undefined terms ('damaged,' 'obvious signs of use,' etc.) create interpretation variability.", "No transparent rubric for calculating fee within the 'up to 50%' range (e.g., what condition warrants 20% vs. 50% fee)."]
Intent: Highlight fee calculation and eligibility assessment ambiguity related to item condition.

policy_clause: Note: Amazon offers a convenient return service for reasonable use by our customers. Should customers abuse this service, we may adjust its availability.
Ambiguity_description: Terms like 'reasonable use' and 'abuse this service' are not defined. Agents (automated or human) lack objective measures for when adjustments (such as return denial or restriction) should be triggered, leading to unpredictability in enforcement.
List_of_ambiguities: ["'Reasonable use' threshold not specified—no clear number or frequency of returns defined.", "'Abuse' is not operationalized, meaning enforcement or restriction criteria are subjective."]
Intent: Expose ambiguity in policy enforcement criteria.

policy_clause: If you return an item that was part of a discount qualification, we will reduce your refund to account for the return.
Ambiguity_description: The policy fails to define how reductions are computed when an item from a bundled or discount-qualified order is returned. No numerical breakdown or method is supplied. Automated refund calculations become unreliable.
List_of_ambiguities: ['The computation for how refunds are adjusted when part of a bundle or discount is returned is not explained.', 'Uncertainty about who is responsible for communicating or justifying the reduced refund.']
Intent: Expose lack of explicit price-adjustment logic.

policy_clause: Please ensure that your item is returned in original or unused condition with tags attached and hygiene seals and liners intact, and in the original manufacturer's packaging including tags, components, accessories, manuals, certificates of authenticity, and other inserts.
Ambiguity_description: It is unclear whether the requirement to include 'tags attached and hygiene seals and liners intact' and 'in the original manufacturer's packaging including tags, components, accessories...' applies to all returned items, or only to certain categories. There is ambiguity about whether failure to comply with any of the sub-requirements disqualifies the return or simply reduces the refund.
List_of_ambiguities: ['Ambiguity regarding which sub-items (tags, components, hygiene seals, etc.) are required for which product categories.', 'Unclear whether missing just one category (e.g., hygiene seal for a non-hygiene product) voids eligibility or results in a partial refund.']
Intent: Highlight logical grouping and applicability issues of return condition requirements.

policy_clause: You can return most items for free at over 8,000 convenient locations, typically within a 5-mile radius of your address.
Ambiguity_description: The phrase 'typically within a 5-mile radius' lacks precision and is not guaranteed, which is problematic for workflow automation that wants to offer customers concrete logistics or for agents managing expectations.
List_of_ambiguities: ["'Typically' is undefined; it is unclear how often or under what conditions the 5-mile radius does not apply.", 'No clarification on alternate processes if no locations are available within 5 miles.']
Intent: Expose uncertainty in logistical promises for in-person returns.

policy_clause: Do not include items from separate orders in the same return.
Ambiguity_description: The policy instructs not to include items from separate orders in the same return, but does not specify what happens if a customer does so, or what the resolution workflow is—leaving escalation and resolution protocols undefined.
List_of_ambiguities: ['No prescriptive action or consequence if customer ignores rule and combines multiple orders in a single return shipment.', 'Lack of process for error correction or communication back to the customer.']
Intent: Highlight procedural ambiguity for non-adherence to return packing rules.

policy_clause: Go to the Returns page. Use the 17-digit order number, for example 123-1234567-1234567 (add: "how do I find this?" hover-over link) on your packing slip that came with your item or on the digital gift receipt, to initiate a return. If you can't locate the order number, ask the gift giver. If you need an exchange or replacement, you'll need to return the gift and place a new order.
Ambiguity_description: For gifts, the instructions do not specify what to do if the gift giver cannot be contacted or does not have the order number. The lack of an alternative pathway for these cases complicates agentic workflows for edge-case support.
List_of_ambiguities: ['No guidance for cases where neither recipient nor giver can locate the order number.', 'No escalation path defined for gifts missing critical identification information.']
Intent: Expose incompleteness in gift return process.
